results.possibleIdentity: Dr. Reginald L. Amory (born July 17, 1936) is a distinguished American civil engineer and educator known for his historical role as the first African American to earn a Ph.D. in engineering from Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute (RPI) in 1967. During the late 1960s, he was an assistant professor of civil engineering at Northeastern University in Boston (1966–1968), where he also served as a consultant and director of structural research for Robert Charles Associates. In 1968, he became the Dean of the School of Engineering at North Carolina A&T State University, a position he held until 1974. He subsequently returned to Northeastern University as the Alcoa Professor of Civil Engineering (1974–1977) and remained on the faculty as a professor until 1992. His technical expertise is in structural engineering and structural mechanics, and he has worked as a consultant for organizations such as the National Science Foundation, the National Academy of Engineering, and Mobil Oil Corporation. Later in his career, he served as the Chair of the Department of Civil Engineering at Morgan State University. He is frequently cited as a pioneer in engineering education, particularly in increasing the representation of African Americans in the field.
